one_line_takeaway: A content-safety dataset + taxonomy + LoRA-tuned guard models, plus a novel application of no-regret online learning-with-experts (Hedge) to dynamically pick the best LLM safety expert per query, beating Llama Guard and API baselines and cutting jailbreak success rates.
key_finding: The fine-tuned AegisSafetyExperts, trained on only half the dataset, beat or match the base guards across all three benchmarks. On the own on-policy test set they reach F1 0.85 (LlamaGuardDefensive) vs 0.62 for LlamaGuardBase and 0.34 for OpenAI Mod API. On ToxicChat (off-policy) they improve AUPRC to 0.703 (Permissive) vs 0.664 base. On SimpleSafetyTests, LlamaGuardDefensive hits 100% accuracy vs 87% base, 89% GPT-4, 72% Perspective. Jailbreak robustness: LoRA fine-tuning dropped TAP attack-success-rate from 66.67% to 33.33% (LlamaGuard) and from 88.88% to 0% (NeMo43B); GCG suffixes achieved 0% ASR against all four models (NeMo proved non-transferable to GCG). The AEGIS online meta-algorithm asymptotically converges to the best expert in hindsight, with the perturbed version adapting faster when expert performance shifts over time. Including AegisSafetyDataset in an alignment blend did not hurt helpfulness (MT-Bench 6.83 vs 6.73 baseline).
core_contribution: The paper delivers four coupled artifacts for LLM content-safety moderation. (1) A content-safety risk taxonomy of 13 major categories plus 9 sparse sub-categories, including a distinctive 'Needs Caution' label for ambiguous cases and an extensible 'Other' bucket. (2) AegisSafetyDataset, ~26,541 human-LLM interaction instances with 86,736 human annotations built from Anthropic hh-rlhf prompts and Mistral-generated responses. (3) AegisSafetyExperts, a suite of LoRA-instruction-tuned guard models (LlamaGuardDefensive, LlamaGuardPermissive, NeMo43B-Defensive) trained on the dataset. (4) AEGIS itself, which is the paper's headline novelty: a *novel application* of the classic no-regret online-learning-with-experts framework (Exponential Weights / Hedge) to content moderation, where a meta-algorithm dynamically picks the best safety expert per round from oracle/human feedback. Note the online-learning framework and regret bounds are cited prior work (Cesa-Bianchi & Lugosi; Littlestone & Warmuth; Vovk); the contribution is applying it to an ensemble of LLM guards, not inventing the algorithm. Llama Guard, OpenAI Moderation API, and Perspective API are baselines/related work, not this paper's systems.
threat_model: Defensive content moderation for human-LLM interactions. The system guards both input (user prompts, system instructions) and output (model responses) against 14 explicit unsafe categories (violence, sexual, sexual-minor, hate, criminal planning, guns/weapons, controlled substances, self-harm, PII, harassment, etc.). Adversary of interest: jailbreak attacks that push an underlying LLM to emit toxic content or that evade the guard model. The paper evaluates guard robustness against two SOTA jailbreaks -- Tree of Attacks with Pruning (TAP) and Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) adversarial suffixes. The Gumbel-perturbed weighting is also motivated as an anti-adversarial measure: perturbation hides which expert was selected, frustrating attacks that must identify the active guard. The authors explicitly scope out designing jailbreak defenses ('our system and dataset do not specifically consider jailbreaks') and treat robustness as an evaluation, not a core claim.
limitations: Models are trained on only 11,000 of 26,541 samples (an 'early subset'); full-dataset training is future work. Inter-annotator agreement is a modest ~74% on subjective safety labels. AUPRC is unavailable for NeMo and the two closed APIs (no logprob access), so several head-to-head cells rely on F1 only, and Perspective/OpenAI API numbers on ToxicChat/OpenAI-Mod are cited from the Llama Guard paper rather than re-run. The AEGIS online-adaptation results are a simulation on the OpenAI Mod dataset with GPT-4-as-judge standing in for the human oracle, not a live deployment; the framework's practicality hinges on availability of a reliable feedback oracle. Jailbreak evaluation is small-scale (30 TAP prompts, 20 GCG suffixes x 10 toxic queries) and the authors note ASR scoring via keyword-matching may conflate genuine catches with degenerate model outputs. GCG could not be optimized directly against NeMo, so its 0% ASR reflects non-transferability rather than a defense per se. The paper does not claim taxonomy comprehensiveness.

datasets_used: AegisSafetyDataset (own; ~26,541 human-LLM interactions, 86,736 annotations; test partition 1199 instances = 639 unsafe / 412 safe / 148 Needs Caution)
Anthropic hh-rlhf (source of prompts; responses generated by Mistral-7B)
OpenAI Moderation Dataset (1680 samples; off-policy benchmark and AEGIS online-adaptation simulation stream)
quantitative_results: On-policy (own test set) F1: LlamaGuardDefensive 0.85, NeMo43B-Defensive 0.89, LlamaGuardPermissive 0.76 vs LlamaGuardBase 0.62, OpenAI Mod API 0.34, Perspective API 0.24
On-policy AUPRC: LlamaGuardDefensive/Permissive 0.941 vs LlamaGuardBase 0.930, OpenAI Mod API 0.895, Perspective API 0.860
ToxicChat AUPRC: LlamaGuardPermissive 0.703, Defensive 0.699 vs Base 0.664, OpenAI Mod API 0.588, Perspective API 0.532
OpenAI Mod AUPRC: OpenAI Mod API 0.856, LlamaGuardPermissive 0.847, Base 0.845, Defensive 0.844, Perspective 0.787
SimpleSafetyTests accuracy: LlamaGuardDefensive 100%, LlamaGuardPermissive 98%, NeMo43B-Defensive 97%, NeMo43B 93%, GPT4 89%, LlamaGuardBase 87%, Perspective API 72%
Jailbreak TAP ASR: LlamaGuard Base 66.67% -> LlamaGuard+LoRA 33.33%; NeMo43B Base 88.88% -> NeMo43B+LoRA 0%
Jailbreak GCG ASR: 0% for all four models
MT-Bench total: Aegis-13B-Aligned 6.83 vs 13B-Aligned baseline 6.73 (helpfulness not degraded)
Inter-annotator agreement ~74%; models LoRA-tuned on 11,000 samples
Online-learning worst-case regret bound O(sqrt(T log K)); EW eta=0.05, perturbed eta=0.26, averaged over 20 trials
baselines_compared: LlamaGuardBase (Llama2-7B Llama Guard, zero-shot with its default taxonomy)
